Composition Key Elements: NdFeB alloy particles and their surface oxides (Nd2O3, Fe2O3). Typical bulk composition: 28-35 wt% Rare Earths (Nd, Pr, Dy, Tb), 60-70 wt% Fe, 0.8-1.2 wt% B.
Generation Rate: One facility reports generating 500 kg/week of "oxidized powder and green body residues". This contributes to overall material yield loss.
Particle Size Or Phase: Fine powder. Typically jet-milled to a mean particle size of 2-5 µm.
Hazard Classification: Pyrophoric/Flammable Solid (UN3089, Class 4.1). Dry powders can oxidize and burn rapidly in air. Powders should be maintained in a water slurry or under an inert atmosphere (N2, Ar) to prevent spontaneous combustion. US RCRA: D001 (Ignitability).
Current Disposal Pathway: Recycled internally or disposed of at a certified waste facility.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Physical Form: Solid (brittle compressed powder). Density is lower than the final sintered magnet, typically 50-60% of theoretical.
Composition Key Elements: Un-sintered NdFeB alloy powder. Composition is the same as the input powder: REE 28-35 wt%, Fe 60-70 wt%, B 0.8-1.2 wt%.
Generation Rate: Contributes to yield loss during the pressing stage. One facility reports a combined generation rate of 500 kg/week for oxidized powder and green body residues.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid pressed compacts ("green bodies") composed of fine (2-5 µm) alloy particles.
Hazard Classification: Solid compacts are stable, but if broken or crushed, the resulting dust has the same pyrophoric/flammable hazards as the source powder (UN3089, Class 4.1). US RCRA: D001 (Ignitability) if in powder form.
Current Disposal Pathway: Recycled internally (e.g., re-blended) or disposed of at a certified waste facility along with oxidized powder.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: Fine NdFeB alloy particles and their oxides. Composition is the same as the parent powder. If cobalt is present, respirable dust (≤10µm) is classified as acutely toxic by inhalation.
Generation Rate: Generated from all powder handling and pressing operations. Dust collectors can have up to 99% removal efficiency for PM10 and PM2.5. One example from steel grinding notes emissions >50 g/h from a single machine.
Particle Size Or Phase: Fine, airborne powder (dust). Particle size is typically in the range of the jet-milled powder, often <10 µm.
Hazard Classification: Flammable Solid, pyrophoric, and poses a dust explosion hazard. Must be handled in inert atmospheres or as a slurry to prevent spontaneous combustion. US RCRA: D001 (Ignitability), D003 (Reactivity).
Current Disposal Pathway: Captured by dust collection systems. Disposed of as hazardous waste or potentially recycled. Spilled dust should be swept and stored in a water slurry or under an inert atmosphere.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: Oxidized deposits of rare earth and iron alloys, potential volatilized components from the sintering process.
Generation Rate: Not mentioned in source. Generated slowly over time.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid deposit, scale, or fused residue.
Hazard Classification: Likely non-hazardous if fully oxidized, but would require TCLP testing to confirm.
Current Disposal Pathway: Mechanically removed (scraped) and disposed of as solid industrial waste, likely to a landfill.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: Vacuum pump oil contaminated with fine NdFeB alloy particles and organic breakdown products.
Generation Rate: Depends on pump size, maintenance schedule, and seal integrity. Not mentioned in source.
Particle Size Or Phase: Liquid (oil) with suspended fine solids.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Classified as used oil in the US and subject to specific RCRA regulations (40 CFR 279). May also be ignitable (D001).
Current Disposal Pathway: Managed as used oil; sent to a certified waste facility for recycling, reprocessing, or incineration.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Disposal cost for used oil is typically in the range of $0-$5 per gallon, but may be higher if contaminated with metal fines.

Composition Key Elements: Sintered NdFeB alloy. Composition is that of the final product grade. Example: Fe 63.54 wt%, Nd 22.21 wt%, Dy 8.19 wt%, Co 2.99 wt%.
Generation Rate: A facility example reported 45 barrels per week of off-spec magnets. Sm-Co magnets can have machining failure rates up to 20% due to brittleness.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid blocks, rings, arcs, or other shapes.
Hazard Classification: Non-hazardous solid article. The primary hazard is physical due to strong magnetic fields and potential for chipping.
Current Disposal Pathway: Typically recycled within the plant. A common method is Hydrogen Decrepitation (HD) to convert the solid chunks back into powder for reprocessing.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Recycled internally, so the cost is that of reprocessing rather than disposal.

Physical Form: Aqueous slurry/sludge. Density can range from 3150-4075 kg/m³ depending on solids loading and composition. Can be dewatered to a filter cake with 25-33% moisture.
Composition Key Elements: Solids: NdFeB alloy particles (e.g., Nd 19.5 wt%, Dy 2.4 wt%, Pr 4.9 wt%, Fe ~60 wt%), abrasive particles (e.g., diamond, SiC). Liquid: Water-based emulsion (5-15% concentrate in water) containing emulsifiers, corrosion inhibitors, biocides. pH is typically alkaline, 9.2-10.0.
Generation Rate: This is a primary waste stream, accounting for a large portion of the 20-30% of material lost during manufacturing. One facility reports generating 16,600 kg/week. A single machine can generate 0.6-1.4 kg/h.
Particle Size Or Phase: Slurry of fine metal particles (e.g., medium diameter 0.5-0.8 mm reported in one study, but typically much finer) suspended in an aqueous emulsion.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. The fine metal powder is pyrophoric when dried. The aqueous slurry can generate flammable hydrogen gas. US RCRA: F006 (if from electroplating surface prep), and/or D-codes for metals (e.g., D007 for Chromium if present). EU EWC: e.g., 12 01 14* (machining sludges containing dangerous substances).
Current Disposal Pathway: Collected and transported off-site for disposal at a certified hazardous waste facility, often a secure landfill after dewatering/stabilization.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source, but hazardous waste landfill costs are significant.

Physical Form: Oily slurry/sludge. Can have high moisture/oil content (e.g., 50%).
Composition Key Elements: Solids: NdFeB alloy particles (e.g., Nd 12.45 wt%, Fe 34.35 wt%, B 0.48 wt%). Liquid: Mineral oil-based coolant containing additives like boric acid (10-20%), ethanol, and amines. Oil content can be >50% of the sludge mass.
Generation Rate: A major contributor to manufacturing waste. More than 70% of total waste can be sludge. Represents a significant portion of the overall 20-30% material loss in manufacturing.
Particle Size Or Phase: Slurry of fine metal particles suspended in oil.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Ignitable due to oil content (US RCRA D001). Flash point of concentrate can be >160°C. Metal powder is pyrophoric upon drying. May be toxic for metals (D-codes). EU EWC: 12 01 10* (synthetic machining oils) or 12 01 07* (mineral-based machining oils).
Current Disposal Pathway: Disposed of at a certified hazardous waste facility, often via incineration or stabilization followed by landfill.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source. Likely "Expensive" category ($15-$50+/gallon) due to mixed hazardous characteristics.

Waste Stream Name: Wire EDM Filter Cake / Dielectric Waste
Physical Form: Solid (filter cake) and liquid (spent dielectric fluid).
Composition Key Elements: Dielectric fluid (deionized water or oil/kerosene), fine NdFeB particles, and particles from the wire electrode (typically brass - Cu/Zn, or molybdenum). Particulate sizes are typically fine, in the range of 1-20 µm.
Generation Rate: Generated from the kerf loss during cutting. Kerf width is typically 250-270 µm. Minimized by using very fine wire, e.g., 0.25 mm (0.010"). Dielectric fluid consumption can be significant, e.g., up to 299.7 mL for one operation in a study.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid filter cake and contaminated liquid. The cake consists of fine (1-20 µm) metallic particles.
Hazard Classification: Depends on the dielectric fluid. Oil-based fluids may be ignitable (D001). The fine metal particles are pyrophoric upon drying. US RCRA: F006 may apply.
Current Disposal Pathway: The dielectric fluid is filtered to remove solid particles. The resulting filter cake and spent fluid are disposed of as hazardous waste.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: NdFeB alloy particles (swarf/chips). Example composition (wt%): Fe 73.71%, RE 25.51%, Ni 0.57%, Cu 0.21%.
Generation Rate: Accounts for 6-73% of material loss during manufacturing steps. One facility reports 1,100 kg/week of combined grinding & sawing materials.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid particles/chips, generally coarser than grinding sludge.
Hazard Classification: Can be classified as non-hazardous if chips are large enough. However, fine particles mixed in are pyrophoric and can spontaneously combust. US RCRA: D001 (Ignitability) if fines are present.
Current Disposal Pathway: Disposed of at a certified waste facility or recycled.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: Carrier fluid (water or oil), fine NdFeB particles, and very fine abrasive particles (e.g., diamond, alumina, colloidal silica). An example magnetic compound fluid (MCF) slurry contained 45 wt% water-based magnetic fluid, 40 wt% iron powder, and 12 wt% Al2O3 abrasive.
Generation Rate: Contributes to overall material loss, which can be 30-40% of the workpiece material. Slurry is consumed/lost via evaporation; one study noted a loss of 0.724 g over 60 minutes of polishing, requiring replenishment of 0.1 mL every 10 minutes.
Particle Size Or Phase: Slurry containing very fine particles (e.g., abrasives at 1 µm, magnetic nanoparticles at 10 nm in some formulations).
Hazard Classification: Hazardous due to fine, pyrophoric metal particles and potentially the carrier fluid. US RCRA: D001 and potentially D-codes for metals.
Current Disposal Pathway: Treated as hazardous waste and sent for disposal at a certified facility.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: Water, base oil, emulsifiers, biocides, corrosion inhibitors, tramp oils (<2%), and dissolved/suspended metals. Microbial concentration can range from 10^4 to 10^10 CFU/mL. Typical operating pH is 9.2-10.0.
Generation Rate: One facility reports generating 25 m³ per week. Coolant is replaced based on rejection criteria like high bacteria count (>10^6 cfu/ml), low pH, or high tramp oil.
Particle Size Or Phase: Liquid (emulsion or solution) with dissolved and suspended fine solids. Oil droplet size is typically 0.2-1.5 microns for emulsions.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. May be classified under various codes depending on contaminants and jurisdiction (e.g., EU EWC 12 01 09* - machining emulsions containing halogens).
Current Disposal Pathway: Sent to a certified hazardous waste facility for specialized treatment such as chemical splitting (emulsion breaking) or evaporation/incineration.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Typically falls into a moderate cost category, around $5-$15 per gallon.

Composition Key Elements: High pH aqueous solution containing sodium hydroxide (NaOH), surfactants, and high concentrations of emulsified oils and greases. Example concentration: 40 g/L for hot dip deoiling solution.
Generation Rate: Generated periodically when the cleaning bath becomes saturated with contaminants and is discarded. Not mentioned in source.
Particle Size Or Phase: Aqueous solution.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Corrosive (US RCRA D002) due to high pH. May also contain listed solvents.
Current Disposal Pathway: Treated on-site in a wastewater treatment plant via neutralization or hauled off-site to a certified hazardous waste facility.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source. Cost is part of overall WWTP OPEX if treated on-site.

Composition Key Elements: Low pH aqueous solution containing nitric acid (HNO3) at concentrations of 1-10% by mass, and high concentrations of dissolved metals (Nd, Fe, etc.). A leachate from scrap can contain 10-20 g/L of Nd and 40-60 g/L of Fe.
Generation Rate: Generated periodically when the acid bath becomes saturated with metals or its effectiveness decreases. Not mentioned in source.
Particle Size Or Phase: Aqueous solution with high dissolved solids.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Corrosive (US RCRA D002) due to low pH. Likely also exhibits toxicity characteristic for metals (D-codes).
Current Disposal Pathway: Treated on-site in a wastewater treatment plant (neutralization, precipitation) or hauled off-site to a certified hazardous waste facility.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source. Cost is part of overall WWTP OPEX if treated on-site.

Composition Key Elements: Aqueous solution with low concentrations of dragged-out plating chemicals, including Ni and Cu ions. An example of copper rinse water has a concentration of <1 g/dm³ (<1000 mg/L).
Generation Rate: Benchmark water usage is 3-20 L/m² of surface area per rinse stage. Total flow depends on production volume and number of rinse stages (typically 3 or more). A facility processing 468,000 m²/yr generated 75 m³/week of wastewater.
Particle Size Or Phase: Aqueous solution containing dissolved metal ions.
Hazard Classification: May be classified as hazardous waste (e.g., US RCRA D006 for cadmium, D007 for chromium, D008 for lead if present) before treatment if metal concentrations exceed TCLP limits. Generally F006 waste once treated.
Current Disposal Pathway: Treated in the on-site wastewater treatment plant via chemical precipitation or ion exchange to recover metals. Treated water may be reused or discharged.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Cost is part of the overall WWTP OPEX. Per-unit treatment costs for metal finishing wastewater can be estimated, but are not specified in the sources.

Composition Key Elements: Concentrated aqueous solution of Nickel Sulfate (240-300 g/L), Nickel Chloride (30-90 g/L), and Boric Acid (30-45 g/L), plus organic brighteners and metallic impurities (e.g., Fe, Cu, Zn). pH: 3.5-4.5. Temperature: 40-60 °C.
Generation Rate: Infrequent. Plating baths typically have a life of several years and are replaced only upon severe contamination or change in process.
Particle Size Or Phase: Concentrated aqueous solution.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Toxic for metals (US RCRA D008 for Nickel). May be corrosive (D002). EU EWC: 11 01 09* (sludges and filter cakes containing dangerous substances).
Current Disposal Pathway: Hauled off-site to a certified hazardous waste facility for metal recovery or secure disposal.
Estimated Disposal Cost: High cost due to high metal concentration and hazardous classification. Falls into "Expensive" category ($15-$50+/gallon).

Composition Key Elements: Concentrated aqueous solution of copper salts and acid. Example composition: Cupric Pyrophosphate 20-120 g/L, Potassium Pyrophosphate 100-300 g/L, additives 0.1-50 g/L. pH: 7.0-10.0.
Generation Rate: Infrequent. Plating baths typically have a life of several years and are only disposed of upon severe contamination.
Particle Size Or Phase: Concentrated aqueous solution.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste. Corrosive (US RCRA D002) and toxic for copper (D006). EU EWC: 11 01 11* (aqueous rinsing liquids containing dangerous substances).
Current Disposal Pathway: Hauled off-site to a certified hazardous waste facility for metal recovery or secure disposal.
Estimated Disposal Cost: High cost due to high metal concentration and hazardous classification. Falls into "Expensive" category ($15-$50+/gallon).

Waste Stream Name: Plating Bath Maintenance Sludge (carbon treatment, anode)
Composition Key Elements: Activated carbon powder or granules saturated with organic brighteners, nickel, copper, and other metallic impurities. Also includes anode sludge containing metal oxides and anode material fines.
Generation Rate: Generated intermittently during bath purification. Activated carbon treatment typically uses 4-8 g/L of bath volume per treatment cycle.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid sludge or spent granular media.
Hazard Classification: Hazardous Waste (US RCRA F006 - sludges from electroplating operations).
Current Disposal Pathway: Disposed of as hazardous waste at a certified facility, typically for metal recovery or secure landfill.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source. Cost would be similar to other hazardous solid wastes.

Composition Key Elements: Cured epoxy resin particles, pigments, and waste organic solvents (e.g., ketones, acetates).
Generation Rate: One facility reports generating 240 gallons/week of residual solvent. Overspray generation depends on transfer efficiency of the coating process.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid powder/flakes (overspray) and liquid (solvents).
Hazard Classification: Solvent waste is typically a hazardous waste, classified as ignitable (D001) and may be a listed waste (e.g., F003, F005). Cured epoxy overspray may be non-hazardous unless contaminated.
Current Disposal Pathway: Solvent waste is typically sent for recovery/recycling or incineration at a certified facility. Solid overspray is disposed of as industrial waste.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source. Solvent disposal costs can be in the $5-$15 per gallon range.

Composition Key Elements: Insoluble metal phosphates, primarily iron phosphate (FePO4) but also zinc and manganese phosphates, formed from the reaction of phosphoric acid with the metal surface and bath constituents.
Generation Rate: Generated continuously at a slow rate as the bath is used; sludge is periodically removed from the tank.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid sludge.
Hazard Classification: Generally considered a non-hazardous waste, unless it fails TCLP tests for leachable heavy metals from the substrate alloy.
Current Disposal Pathway: Typically dewatered and disposed of in an industrial landfill.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Not mentioned in source.

Composition Key Elements: NdFeB alloy magnet with a surface coating. Composition is that of the finished product.
Generation Rate: Not mentioned in source. Depends on final QC standards and process control.
Particle Size Or Phase: Solid parts of various geometries (disc, block, arc, etc.).
Hazard Classification: Non-hazardous solid waste. Physical hazard from strong magnetic fields.
Current Disposal Pathway: Often sold for scrap metal value to recyclers who may or may not recover the rare earth elements. Some may be landfilled if no recycling outlet is available.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Can have a positive value as scrap, otherwise landfill costs apply.

Physical Form: Solid filter cake. Achievable solids concentration is typically 30-55% for metal hydroxide sludge from a filter press. Membrane presses can achieve up to 75% solids.
Composition Key Elements: Hydrated hydroxides of iron, nickel, copper, and rare earths (Nd, Dy, Pr). Also contains calcium sulfate or carbonate if lime is used for precipitation. Example plating sludge composition (g/kg dry): Zn 198, Fe 129, CaO 40. Contains soil, oil, and grease from upstream processes.
Generation Rate: Depends on wastewater flow rate and contaminant loading. An example plating plant produces 30 tonnes/year (60% moisture), while another produces 60 tonnes/year (70% solid content). The entire STM sector in the EU produces about 300,000 tonnes/year.
Particle Size Or Phase: Dewatered solid cake. Initial precipitates can be nano-sized (~25 nm) but flocculate into larger aggregates.
Hazard Classification: US RCRA: F006 (Wastewater treatment sludges from electroplating operations). May also exhibit toxicity characteristics (D-codes) for various metals. EU EWC: 11 01 09* (sludges and filter cakes containing dangerous substances).
Current Disposal Pathway: Transported to a licensed hazardous waste landfill for secure disposal, or sent to a metal reclaimer for recovery of valuable metals like Ni and Cu.
Estimated Disposal Cost: Recycling costs can be around EUR 165/tonne (~$180/tonne). Landfill costs vary significantly by region, but can be in the range of $300-$1000+ per tonne for hazardous waste.
